fragments.content: Half-life (t1/2) is the time required for plasma drug concentration to decrease by 50% during the elimination phase. It reflects the combined effects of volume of distribution and clearance: t1/2 = (0.693 × Vd) / CL. Most drugs follow first-order kinetics where a constant fraction is eliminated per unit time, making half-life constant regardless of concentration. After one half-life, 50% remains; after two, 25%; after five, ~3% (considered effectively eliminated). Half-life determines dosing frequency and time to steady state (reached after 4-5 half-lives). Drugs with short half-lives require frequent dosing or extended-release formulations; long half-life drugs accumulate and take longer to reach steady state.

Clinical applications of half-life guide dosing interval selection and predict drug accumulation. Dosing interval typically equals half-life for maintenance therapy. Loading dose achieves therapeutic levels immediately when half-life is long (e.g., digoxin t1/2=36h, amiodarone t1/2=58 days). Time to steady state = 4-5 half-lives regardless of dose size; increasing dose accelerates achievement of target concentration but not steady state timing. When discontinuing drugs: those with short half-lives may cause withdrawal (alprazolam t1/2=11h); long half-life drugs taper themselves (fluoxetine t1/2=4-6 days, active metabolite 7-15 days). Renal/hepatic impairment prolongs half-life requiring dose reduction or interval extension.

Half-life is calculated from the terminal elimination phase of the concentration-time curve: t1/2 = 0.693 / ke (elimination rate constant). Determined by plotting log concentration vs time and measuring slope of terminal phase. Context-sensitive half-life applies to IV infusions: time for 50% decrease after stopping infusion, which increases with infusion duration for drugs with multi-compartment kinetics (e.g., fentanyl, propofol). Effective half-life may differ from elimination half-life for drugs with active metabolites or non-linear kinetics. Zero-order kinetics (constant amount eliminated per time, not fraction) occurs with saturable metabolism (phenytoin, ethanol) where half-life increases with concentration.
